Compound Words (Σύνθετες Λέξεις)

Greek vocabulary is massive, but it's incredibly logical. Instead of inventing brand new words for every concept, Greeks build words like Lego bricks by snapping smaller words together.

Once you understand how the bricks connect, you can usually guess the meaning of a C1/C2 word even if you've never seen it before!

These Lego-block words are called Compound Words.

1. The Greek "Glue": The letter Omikron (ο)

When you attach two Greek words together, you almost always need "glue" to connect them.

In Greek, the glue is the letter ο (omikron). If the first word ends in a normal vowel (like -ος or -η), that vowel simply turns into an ο. Then the second word attaches to it!

Example 1: The Bookstore

  • βιβλίο (book) + πωλώ (I sell)
  • βιβλίο ➔ βιβλι- + glue ο ➔ βιβλιο-
  • βιβλιο- + -πωλείο (selling place) = βιβλιοπωλείο (bookstore).

Example 2: The Fish Tavern

  • ψάρι (fish) + ταβέρνα (tavern)
  • ψάρι ➔ ψαρ- + glue ο ➔ ψαρο-
  • ψαρο- + ταβέρνα = ψαροταβέρνα (fish tavern).

2. Prefixes (The Power Bricks)

There are dozens of highly active prefixes (mini-words) that you can slap onto the front of any word to instantly completely change its meaning.

Ξανα- (Again / Re-)

If you want to say "do it again" or "re-do", just add ξανα- to the verb! - κάνω (I do) ➔ ξανακάνω (I re-do) - βλέπω (I see) ➔ ξαναβλέπω (I re-watch / see again) - λέω (I say) ➔ ξαναλέω (I repeat)

Α- / Αν- (The Negative / Un-)

This is the famous Alpha Privative. In English, we use "un-" or "in-" (unhappy, invisible). Greek uses a simple α- (if the next letter is a consonant) or αν- (if the next letter is a vowel). - γνωστός (known) ➔ άγνωστος (unknown) - δίκαιος (fair/just) ➔ άδικος (unfair) - ικανός (capable) ➔ ανίκανος (incapable) - ηθικός (ethical) ➔ ανήθικος (unethical)

Ξε- (The Reverser / Un-)

This is the action reverser. While the Alpha Privative makes adjectives negative, ξε- is used to reverse the action of a verb (like tying and un-tying). - κλειδώνω (I lock) ➔ ξεκλειδώνω (I unlock) - δένω (I tie) ➔ ξεδένω (I untie) - βάφω (I paint) ➔ ξεβάφω (I un-paint / strip paint)

3. Creating Adjectives from Nouns

You can stick two nouns together to create a powerful descriptive adjective. Always remember to use the glue ο!

  • χρώμα (color) + ασήμι (silver) = ασημόχρωμος (silver-colored).
  • καρδιά (heart) + πέτρα (stone) = πετρόκαρδος (stone-hearted).
  • κεφάλι (head) + ελαφρύ (light) = ελαφροκέφαλος (light-headed).

4. Famous Greek Roots Every Learner Must Know

Once you hit C1, you will see these roots everywhere:

  1. -φίλος (Lover / Friend):
    - ζώο (animal) + φίλος = ζωόφιλος (animal lover). - ξένος (foreigner) + φίλος = φιλόξενος (hospitable).

  2. -φόβος (Fearer / Hater): - ξένος + φόβος = ξενοφοβία (fear of foreigners). - ύψος (height) + φόβος = υψοφοβία (fear of heights).

  3. -λόγος (Speaker / Studier of): - ψυχή (soul) + λόγος = ψυχολόγος (psychologist).

🎯 Wrap-Up Checklist

  • [ ] I know that Greek builds words by snapping them together using the letter ο as "glue".
  • [ ] I can reverse an action using ξε- (ξεκλειδώνω).
  • [ ] I can do something repeatedly using ξανα- (ξαναλέω).
  • [ ] I know that adding an α- or αν- negates an adjective (γνωστός -> άγνωστος).
